title: Psalms 71
subtitle: Do Not Cast Me Away When I Am Old

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “You Have Been My Hope Since My Youth”

heb.paragraph: "Since my youth (minnəʿūray), God, you have taught me, and to this day I declare your marvellous deeds" (v.17). An elder’s psalm — the speaker looks back on a lifetime of God’s faithfulness. From the womb (v.6), from youth (v.5), to old age (v.9) and grey hairs (v.18). The plea: do not cast me away when I am old and my strength is gone (v.9). The enemies say "God has forsaken him" (v.11). The elder replies: you have been my hope, my confidence, my praise from birth.

notes.note: "You have been my hope, Sovereign LORD, my confidence since my youth. From birth I have relied on you." A lifetime of trust: from womb to present. The elder’s testimony spans decades.
"Do not cast me away when I am old; do not forsake me when my strength is gone." The fear unique to ageing: becoming useless, being abandoned. The plea is for continuity of care.
"They say, ‘God has forsaken him.’" The cruelest taunt against the elderly believer: you trusted God all your life and now look at you.

refs.note: Trust from the womb and reliance on God from birth parallels this psalm's lifelong dependence.
God carrying Israel from birth to old age directly echoes the psalm's plea for aged care.
Outwardly wasting away while inwardly renewed addresses the same aging vulnerability.

hist.context: Psalm 71 is an elderly person's prayer, unique in the Psalter for its focus on old age. It lacks a superscription but borrows heavily from Psalms 22, 31, and 35, suggesting compilation from earlier materials. The repeated plea 'do not cast me away when I am old' (v.9) voices the ancient fear of abandonment when strength fails and enemies see opportunity.

sections.header: Verses 14–24 — “Even When I Am Old and Grey”

heb.paragraph: "My mouth will tell of your righteous deeds (ṣidqāṭək̅ā), of your saving acts all day long" (v.15). The elder’s mission: to declare God’s power to the next generation (v.18). "Even when I am old and grey, do not forsake me, my God, till I declare your power to the next generation" (v.18). Old age has a purpose: testimony. The grey-haired generation carries the story forward. "You have done great things; who is like you, O God?" (v.19).

notes.note: "Since my youth, God, you have taught me. Even when I am old and grey, do not forsake me till I declare your power to the NEXT GENERATION." The elder’s purpose: bridge. They connect past faithfulness to future faith.
"You have done great things. Though you have made me see troubles, many and bitter, you will restore my life again." The honest testimony: troubles were many AND God was faithful. Both are true.
"I will praise you with the harp; my lips will shout for joy; my tongue will tell of your righteous acts." The elder worships: harp, shout, tongue. Old age does not silence praise — it deepens it.

refs.note: The righteous flourishing in old age and bearing fruit parallels this psalm's commitment to lifelong praise.
Older believers teaching the next generation fulfills the psalm's vow to declare God's power to future generations.
Christ exalted in the body whether by life or death echoes the psalm's resolve to praise continually.

hist.context: The vow to 'tell of your righteous acts all day long' (v.24) and declare God's power 'to the next generation' (v.18) frames aging as opportunity for witness rather than decline. The psalm transforms vulnerability into vocation: the aged become repositories of testimony. The reference to troubles 'many and bitter' (v.20) suggests a life reviewed with hard-won perspective.

ppl.text: ’" The cruelest taunt against the elderly believer: you trusted God all your life and now look at you…

rec.text: ""You have been my hope, Sovereign LORD, my confidence since my youth. From birth I have relied on you." A lifetime of trust: from womb to present. The elder’s testimony spans decades."

rows.label: The Elder’s Fear — 71:1–13
rows.text: "Do not cast me away when I am old." From womb to grey hairs. A lifetime of trust.

hebtext.note: "Since my youth (minnəʿūray), God, you have taught me, and to this day I declare your marvellous deeds" (v.17). An elder’s psalm — the speaker looks back on a lifetime of God’s faithfulness. From the womb (v.6), from youth (v.5), to old age (v.9) and grey hairs (v.18). The plea: do not cast me away when I am old and my strength is gone (v.9). The enemies say "God has forsaken him" (v.11). The elder replies: you have been my hope, my confidence, my praise from birth.

"My mouth will tell of your righteous deeds (ṣidqāṭək̅ā), of your saving acts all day long" (v.15). The elder’s mission: to declare God’s power to the next generation (v.18). "Even when I am old and grey, do not forsake me, my God, till I declare your power to the next generation" (v.18). Old age has a purpose: testimony. The grey-haired generation carries the story forward. "You have done great things; who is like you, O God?" (v.19).

themes.note: Faith peaks: a lifetime of trust. Mission: the elder’s purpose is intergenerational testimony. Worship: old age deepens praise.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Do not cast me away when I am old,' consider asking God whether you fear irrelevance as you age — and whether you trust that He has purpose for every season of life, including the ones where your strength diminishes.
